title: 2 Thessalonians: Stand Firm
objective: Discern end-times confusion and practice steadfast, disciplined faithfulness.

required_passages.text: Stand firm... do not be quickly shaken... the Lord is faithful... if anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat... do not grow weary in doing good.

segments.audio_script: Paul corrects fear and disorder by grounding believers in truth and perseverance.

Listen to 2 Thessalonians. Paul encourages persecuted believers with God's justice and Christ's return. He clarifies that the day of the Lord has not already come and warns against deception. He calls the church to stand firm in apostolic teaching and practice disciplined, responsible life.
False reports and idle behavior destabilized the church's hope and witness.
Eschatology should stabilize, not sensationalize. Truth and disciplined work protect community health while believers await Christ.
Key themes include perseverance, discernment, faithfulness, discipline, and patient hope.

Review question: What does Paul say about the man of lawlessness in 2 Thessalonians 2? Answer: he will exalt himself above every god and object of worship, proclaiming himself to be God, before the Lord Jesus destroys him at his coming.
segments.correct_answer: he exalts himself above God and claims to be God — destroyed by the breath of Christ's mouth at his coming
acceptable_variants: the man of lawlessness claims to be god and is destroyed by christ's return
2 thessalonians 2 — the antichrist figure defeated by the lord at his appearance
he sets himself up as god in the temple before the lord's coming destroys him

segments.audio_script: Stand firm in truth and do not grow weary in good. Next, the pastoral letters train church leaders for healthy doctrine and life.

vocabulary.definition: The figure in 2 Thessalonians 2 who opposes and exalts himself against every god; he deceives many before being destroyed by Christ's coming — often identified with the antichrist of 1 John

The unnamed power in 2 Thessalonians 2:6-7 that currently prevents the man of lawlessness from being revealed; its identity is debated — interpretations include the Roman Empire, the Holy Spirit, or Michael
